coredata.dc:title: A novel photoelectrochemical biosensor for the sensitive detection of dual microRNAs using molybdenum carbide nanotubes as nanocarriers and energy transfer between CQDs and AuNPs
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Minghui
Yin, Huanshun
Zhou, Yunlei
Meng, Xiangjian
Waterhouse, Geoffrey I.N.
Ai, Shiyun
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a novel photoelectrochemical (PEC) biosensor was developed for the ultrasensitive detection of dual microRNAs (miRNAs), with the detection being based on energy transfer (ET) between carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). The PEC platform consisted of a CQDs@Mo2C nanotube modified ITO electrode. Two hairpin probes (H1 and H2) carrying the Au NPs were used “switch off” and “switch on” the PEC signal of the CQDs, with a close approach of the tagged AuNPs to the CQDs quenching the PEC signal. The introduction of different miRNAs (miRNA-159b and miRNA-166a) altered the interparticle distance between the AuNPs and CQDs, thereby affecting the intensity of the PEC response. This approach allowed the highly sensitive detection of both miRNA-159b and miRNA-166a. The linear range of the biosensor for miRNA-159b and miRNA-166a detection were 0.5–5000 fM, with low detection limits of 0.15 fM and 0.21 fM, respectively. To our knowledge, this is the first reported CQDs-based ET biosensor for the PEC detection of dual miRNAs. Results suggest that this approach offers a promising platform for the ultrasensitive detection of multiple miRNAs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs@Mo2C
AuNPs
Energy transfer
MicroRNA detection
Photoelectrochemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Au NPs and Au NPs-labelled hairpin probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) CQDs and (B) Mo2C nanotubes. (C) SEM image of Mo2C nanotubes. (D) TEM image of the CQDs@Mo2C composite. (E) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the AuNPs (a) and PL emission spectrum of the CQDs (b). (F) UV–vis absorption spectra of AuNPs (a) and Au labeled hairpin probe (b). The insets show a TEM image of the AuNPs.

(A) EIS curves for (a) bare ITO, (b) CQDs@Mo2C/ITO, (c) H1/CQDs@Mo2C/ITO, (d) miRNA-159b/H1/CQDs@Mo2C/ITO, (e) miRNA-159b/H1/CQDs@Mo2C/ITO after incubation with DSN, (f) Au-probe/miRNA-159b /H1/CQDs/Mo2C/ITO. (B) The photocurrent response of (a) CQDs@Mo2C/ITO, (b) H1/CQDs@Mo2C/ITO, (c) miRNA-159b/H1/CQDs@Mo2C/ITO, (d) miRNA-159b/H1/CQDs@Mo2C/ITO after incubation with DSN, (e) Au-probe/miRNA-159b /H1/CQDs/Mo2C/ITO.

(A) Photocurrent response of the PEC biosensor to different concentrations of miRNA-159b. a-i, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 500, 1000, 5000 fM (The concentration of miRNA-166a was fixed at 0.5 fM). (B) Calibration curve of miRNA-159b analysis. Error bars represent the standard deviation of triplicate measurements. (C) Photocurrent responses of the PEC biosensor to different concentrations of miRNA-166a. a-i, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 500, 1000, 5000 fM (The concentration of miRNA-159b was fixed at 5 pM). (D) Calibration curve of miRNA-166a analysis. Error bars represent the standard deviation of triplicate measurements.

(A) Selectivity of the biosensor when analyzed with (a) miRNA-162b, (b) miRNA-395b, (c) miRNA-164d, (d) miRNA-156b, (e) miRNA-159b, and (f) mixture of miRNA-159b, miRNA-162b, miRNA-395b, miRNA-164d, miRNA-156b. (B) Selectivity of the biosensor when analyzed with (a) miRNA-162b, (b) miRNA-395b, (c) miRNA-164d, (d) miRNA-156b, (e) miRNA-166a, and (f) mixture of miRNA-166a, miRNA-162b, miRNA-395b, miRNA-164d, miRNA-156b (The concentration of microRNAs is 5 pM). (C) The reproducibility of the fabricated biosensor. (D) Time-based photocurrent response of the biosensor.
Schematic illustration showing the fabrication and operating mechanism of the novel PEC biosensor for dual microRNA detection based on electron transfer between CQDs and AuNPs.
Comparison of various methods for the detection of miRNA.

head.ce:title: A novel photoelectrochemical biosensor for the sensitive detection of dual microRNAs using molybdenum carbide nanotubes as nanocarriers and energy transfer between CQDs and AuNPs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University, Taian 271018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, The University of Auckland, Auckland 1142, New Zealand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a novel photoelectrochemical (PEC) biosensor was developed for the ultrasensitive detection of dual microRNAs (miRNAs), with the detection being based on energy transfer (ET) between carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). The PEC platform consisted of a CQDs@Mo2C nanotube modified ITO electrode. Two hairpin probes (H1 and H2) carrying the Au NPs were used “switch off” and “switch on” the PEC signal of the CQDs, with a close approach of the tagged AuNPs to the CQDs quenching the PEC signal. The introduction of different miRNAs (miRNA-159b and miRNA-166a) altered the interparticle distance between the AuNPs and CQDs, thereby affecting the intensity of the PEC response. This approach allowed the highly sensitive detection of both miRNA-159b and miRNA-166a. The linear range of the biosensor for miRNA-159b and miRNA-166a detection were 0.5–5000 fM, with low detection limits of 0.15 fM and 0.21 fM, respectively. To our knowledge, this is the first reported CQDs-based ET biosensor for the PEC detection of dual miRNAs. Results suggest that this approach offers a promising platform for the ultrasensitive detection of multiple miRNAs.

ce:section.ce:para: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are endogenous and noncoding RNAs (typical length around 22 nucleotides) that play a crucial role in gene regulation, cell development, metabolic processes and apoptosis [1,2]. Due to their low concentration, high sequence homology and easy degradability, the detection of miRNAs is very challenging. To date, a wide range of methods have been explored for microRNA detection, including northern blotting [3], microarrays [4], fluorescence [5], electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [6–8], photoelectrochemical (PEC) [9,10] and electrochemical [11–14] methods. Whilst all of these methods can be used for miRNA detection, they differ considerably in their sensitivity and analysis times. Amongst these, PEC biosensors represent one of the most promising technologies due to their excellent sensitivity, low background noise and easy operation.

Energy transfer (ET) is a powerful strategy for monitoring specific target molecules during electrochemiluminescence and fluorescence assays [15,16]. This has prompted the development of various ET-based PEC platforms for sensing applications [17]. In the ET-based PEC sensors, overlap between the emission spectrum of quantum dots (QDs) and the absorption spectrum of noble metal nanoparticles (NPs) causes excitation of the plasmon resonance of the NPs, resulting in the generation of intense local electric fields around the NPs that perturb the exciton states in the QDs, resulting in a photocurrent reduction [18,19]. Xu’s group exploited ET between CdS QDs and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) in the sensitive detection of DNA [20]. Based on energy transfer between CdSe QDs and AuNPs, a highly sensitive PEC method was developed for monitoring DNA methyltransferase activity and inhibitor screening [21]. Through direct exciton-plasmon interactions between CdS QDs and AgNPs, a novel PEC biosensor was developed for the sensitive detection of miRNA [22]. These works illustrate the excellent performance that is achievable using ET-based PEC systems. However, research in the area of ET-based PEC assays is still very much in its infancy. Typically, the QDs used in most existing works are cadmium chalcogenides (CdS or CdSe), which are cytotoxic and have the potential to cause environmental pollution (when discarded after the ET-based PEC analyses). Accordingly, it is highly desirable to develop to ET-based PEC platforms and biosensors based on environmentally friendly QDs.

In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as a very promising, low cost alternative to traditional CdS or CdSe QDs [23,24]. CQDs offer the advantages of high photostability, good biocompatibility, non-toxicity and easy functionalization [25–27]. The successful application of CQDs in fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) has already been documented [28,29]. However, the PEC signal of CQDs is relatively weak due to their modest electrical conductivity, which is an obstacle to their direct application in PEC devices. To improve the PEC behavior, combining CQDs with other nanomaterials that possess excellent electron transfer properties is expected to be highly beneficial, though little work has been reported in this area to date.

Recently, transition metal carbides (TMCs) have attracted great attention due to their outstanding thermal properties and metal-like electrical conductivity, the latter being attributed to the unique metal-C chemical bonding which creates metal-like d-states around the Fermi level [30,31]. Nanostructured molybdenum carbide (Mo2C) is a particularly promising TMC due to its high stability, low cost and excellent electrical conductivity [32–34], which has resulted in applications in catalysis [35], supercapacitors [36], semiconductors [37] and immunosensing [38]. For example, Wang's group used a Mo2C nanocomposite as a nanocarrier of an ECL probe, which was then used to construct a simple ECL immunosensor for the sensitive detection of α-fetoprotein [39]. In view of its excellent electrical conductivity, nanostructured Mo2C was expected to be a near ideal carrier for CQDs, thereby allowing the potential of CQDs to be fully exploited in PEC applications.

Inspired by the above, we developed herein a novel PEC biosensor for the highly sensitive detection of dual microRNAs. The biosensor coupled duplex specific nuclease (DSN)-assisted signal amplification with the energy transfer between CQDs (amino-functionalized) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). Mo2C nanotubes were successfully employed as nanocarriers of the CQDs. Scheme 1 depicts the fabrication and operating mechanism of the PEC biosensor. AuNPs were brought within close proximity to the CQDs@Mo2C modified ITO electrode through the hairpin probe H1. At this point, the PEC signal was quenched due to the ET between the CQDs and AuNPs. In the presence of miRNA-159b, a DNA-RNA duplex was formed and the stem of H1 was untied, which moved the AuNPs further away from the electrode surface. At this point, the biosensor switched to the “signal on” mode. DSN only cleaves DNA in the DNA-RNA duplex, thus part of H1 is cleaved off resulting in the release of miRNA-159b. Hybridization of miRNA-159b with another H1 initiates a new cycle of signal amplification. In a separate solution, miRNA-166a hybridizes with the hairpin probe H2 to form a DNA-RNA duplex for DSN cleavage (freeing miRNA-166a to hybridize with another H2). The cleavage also generates the Au-probe, which is complementary to the immobilized part of H1 remaining on the CQDs@Mo2C modified ITO electrode. After the Au-probe attached to the electrode, the PEC signal was again quenched due to ET between the CQDs and Au NPs. By this strategy, an increase of miRNA-159b would lead to an enhancement of the PEC signal, and an increase of miRNA-166a would lead to quenching of the signal. The developed biosensor enabled highly sensitive detection of miRNA-159b and miRNA-166a. To our knowledge, this is the first study to report a CQD-based ET biosensor for the PEC detection of dual microRNAs.

Duplex-specific nuclease (DSN) was obtained from Evrogen Joint Stock Company (Moscow, Russia). 3-Mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) was sourced from Alfa Aesar (UK). Tris(2-carboxyethyl) phosphine hydrochloride (TCEP), tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris), N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide ester (NHS) and chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). HPLC-purified miRNAs and the synthetic DNA were provided by Takara Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Dalian, China) and Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), respectively. The DNA and miRNA were stored in TE buffer at −20 °C before use. The sequences of oligonucleotides were shown in Table S1 (Supplementary material). All other reagents were of analytical grade. The various buffers used in this work are described in the Supplementary material.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a Tecnai G2 F20 instrument (USA). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were acquired on a Quanta Q400 (USA). Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was performed on a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Varian, USA). UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained on a UV-3600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). PEC measurements were carried out on a homemade PEC system with a 500 W Xe lamp as the irradiation source (Beijing CeAuLight Technology Co., Ltd., China). Photocurrent measurements were performed on a CHI832A electrochemical workstation (Austin, USA) using a three-electrode system. A modified ITO electrode (0.195 cm2) was used as the working electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode and a Pt wire as the counter electrode. The photocurrent was recorded in the PEC detection buffer under visible light irradiation with an applied potential of −0.3 V. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out on a CHI660C electrochemical workstation (CHI Instruments, Austin, TX).

Mo2C nanotubes were prepared according to a literature method [38]. In brief, (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O (1.4 g) was first dissolved in 40 mL of an aqueous HNO3 solution (11 vol% HNO3). Subsequently, the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 20 h. The product (MoO3 nanorods) was collected by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 15 min) and washed several times with water and ethanol, and then dried at 60 °C for 12 h.

The as-prepared MoO3 nanorods were then used to prepare Mo2C nanotubes. Briefly, MoO3 nanorods (100 mg) were dispersed into 20 mL of deionized H2O and ultrasonically treated for 15 min. Then, (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O (200 mg) and dopamine hydrochloride (50 mg) were dissolved in the dispersion. Subsequently, 40 mL of ethanol was added to the dispersion under stirring, after stirring for five min, 300 μL of 28–30% aqueous ammonia added into the dispersion under gentle stirring. After two hours, the orange-red solid product (Mo2C nanotubes) was collected by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 15 min), washed with ethanol several times and finally dried under vacuum at 60 °C.

Amino-functionalized CQDs were prepared according to literature method [29]. Briefly, D-glucosamine hydrochloride (75 mM, 2 mL) was added into 28 mL of a sodium pyrophosphate solution (0.938 mM) under vigorous stirring, and the stirring continued for 10 min. Then, 25 mL of the obtained solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the yellow–brown CQDs suspension was centrifuged (12000 rpm, 30 min) and the upper yellow-brown layer collected and dialyzed (3 kDa, 12 h), before storage at 4 °C for later use.

The CQDs@Mo2C nanocomposite was prepared by adding a Mo2C dispersion (2.0 mg mL−1) dropwise into the CQDs dispersion under vigorous shaking. After shaking overnight, the composite was collected by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 15 min) and washed with water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Au NPs and Au NPs-labelled hairpin probe
ce:section.ce:para: AuNPs with an average diameter of 5 ± 1 nm were prepared according to a literature method, with a few modifications [22]. Briefly, ice cold NaBH4 (0.1 M, 0.6 mL) was added dropwise to 20 mL of a 0.25 mM HAuCl4 solution under constant stirring, whereupon the solution immediately turned orange-red. After stirring for 10 min in the ice bath and for another three hours at room temperature, the wine-red colored Au NP dispersion was obtained. The dispersion of AuNPs was stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C until use.

For the preparation of the AuNPs-labelled hairpin probes, 100 μL of 10 μM hairpin probe was mixed with 20 µL of 10 mM TCEP and the resulting mixture incubated for one hour at room temperature. Afterwards, 2 mL of the AuNPs dispersion was added and the resulting dispersion incubated for 12 h at room temperature under gentle stirring. During this incubation, 800 µL of NaCl (0.5 M) was gently added. The resulting dispersion was centrifuged, then washed and kept in 10 mM PBS containing 0.1 M NaCl (pH 7.4). The product was the AuNPs-labeled hairpin probe solution.

The amplification reactions were carried out in a solution comprising 100 μL of reaction mixture containing 10 μM H2, various concentration of miRNA-166a, 1 × DSN buffer and 0.5 U DSN. The mixture was incubated at 65 °C for 60 min. Afterwards, 30 μL EDTA (10 mM) was added into the mixture and incubated at 65 °C for five min to devitalize the DSN enzyme.

ITO conductive glass was first cut into slices (5 × 1 cm2) and then sonicated in an ethanol/NaOH solution (v/v, 1:1) for 0.5 h, followed by sonication in acetone for 0.5 h and then doubly distilled water for 0.5 h before drying at room temperature. Subsequently, 40 μL of an aqueous dispersion of CQDs@Mo2C was dropped onto the bare ITO electrode and dried under an infrared lamp. The obtained CQDs@Mo2C/ITO electrode was rinsed with double distilled water three times.

Next, 100 μL of the AuNPs-H1 dispersion was activated by mixing with 100 μL of 0.1 M PBS containing NHS (0.5 mg/mL) and EDC (0.5 mg/mL) for two hours. Then, 40 μL of the obtained AuNPs-H1 solution was added to the CQDs@Mo2C/ITO electrode and then the resulting modified electrode incubated for two hours to immobilize the AuNPs-H1 on the electrode. The obtained electrode was rinsed with washing buffer and denoted as H1/CQDs@Mo2C/ITO. Then, 40 μL of 0.1 M PBS containing 1.0 mM MPA was dropped on the H1/CQDs@Mo2C/ITO, and the electrode incubated for 0.5 h followed by rinsing with washing buffer.

Then, 40 μL of miRNA hybridization buffer containing different concentrations of miRNA-159b was dropped onto the modified electrode and incubated for two hours (to obtain miRNA-159b/H1/CQDs@Mo2C/ITO). Then, miRNA-159b/H1/CQDs@Mo2C/ITO was incubated with 40 μL DSN reaction mixture containing 0.5 U DSN and 1 × DSN reaction buffer for one hour at 65 °C. After rinsing with washing buffer, the electrode was then incubated with 40 μL of amplification products for one hour. Finally, the obtained electrode (noted as Au-probe/miRNA-159b/H1/CQDs@Mo2C/ITO) was rinsed with washing buffer and prepared for PEC test.

Fig. 1 A and B show representative TEM images of the CQDs and Mo2C nanotubes, respectively. The CQDs were near spherical with an average size of 4 ± 1 nm. The Mo2C nanotubes possessed a hierarchical structure with outer diameters in the range 400–800 nm and wall thicknesses around 100 nm (Fig. 1B). The SEM image of the Mo2C nanotubes (Fig. 1C) reveals that the walls of the nanotubes are constructed form 2D ultrathin nanosheets. The TEM image of CQDs@Mo2C (Fig. 1D) shows the presence of CQDs on Mo2C nanotubes, confirming the successful surface functionalization of the nanotubes with CQDs. Fig. 1E shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the AuNPs (curve a) and the PL spectrum of the CQDs (curve b). It could be observed that the maximum absorption peak of AuNPs was centered at 520 nm, very close to the emission maximum of the CQDs (500 nm). The considerable spectral overlap between the emission band of the CQDs and the absorption band of the AuNPs is near ideal for achieving interparticle energy transfer, a fact which is exploited below in PEC biosensor development. After attachment to the hairpin probe, the characteristic peak of the Au NPs red-shifted slightly to 527 nm (Fig. 1F), which is either due to the increases in the dielectric constant of the medium surrounding the Au NPs or partial aggregation of AuNPs induced by the hairpin probe [40]. TEM showed the AuNPs were spherical with an average particle size of 5 ± 1 nm (Fig. 1F, inset). The results of Fig. 1 confirm the successful fabrication of all the nanomaterial building blocks required for PEC biosensor assembly.

In order to provide additional evidence of the formation of the CQDs@Mo2C composite, the FT-IR spectra of Mo2C nanotubes, CQDs@Mo2C composites and CQDs were recorded in Fig. S2. (Supplementary material)

The stepwise fabrication process of the biosensor was followed by electrochemical impedence spectroscopy (EIS) in 10 mM Tris-HCl containing 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution and 0.1 M KCl (pH 7.4). As shown in Fig. 2 A, the bare ITO electrode exhibited an electron transfer resistance (R et) of about 280 Ω. The R et decreased significantly when CQDs@Mo2C was deposited on the ITO electrode surface (curve b), which is explained by the high specific surface area and excellent electrical conductivity of Mo2C. When H1 (curve c) was attached to the CQDs@Mo2C/ITO electrode, a significant increase in R et was observed due to electrostatic repulsion between the negatively charged phosphoric acid backbone of H1 and the negatively charged [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. The R et value increased further when the H1/CQDs@Mo2C/ITO electrode was incubated with miRNA-159b (curve d). This increase can be attributed to the formation of duplex structures, which contain even more negative phosphate groups, thereby hindering the electron transfer. After the electrode was incubated with DSN (curve e), the R et decreased considerably, since DSN cleaves off part of H1 resulting in the release of miRNA-159b. Finally, the R et increased when the Au-probe was immobilized on the electrode (curve f), indicating that the duplex structure had formed which blocked the electron transfer. The EIS data for the different modified electrodes confirms the successful fabrication of the PEC biosensor.

The fabrication of the biosensor was also monitored using the photocurrent response, as illustrated in Fig. 2B. Compared with the CQDs@Mo2C modified electrode (curve a), the immobilization of H1 (curve b) caused a significant decrease in the photocurrent. This is explained by the quenching effect of the AuNPs on the CQDs PEC response. After hybridized with miRNA-159b (curve c), the PEC signal increased, which is attributed to the formation of linear DNA which moves the AuNPs away from the electrode surface and eliminates the ET quenching. A further signal increase was observed after the electrode was incubated with DSN (curve d), which can be understood by DSN-assisted target recycling amplification, which made AuNPs moving away from the electrode continuously. Finally, the photocurrent response decreased dramatically when the Au-probe (derived from H2) was assembled on the electrode due to the energy transfer between the CQDs and AuNPs quenching the signal. Thus, like the EIS, the PEC data confirmed that each PEC biosensor fabrication step was successful.

To improve the detection sensitivity and efficiency of the biosensor, several parameters were optimized, such as Mo2C concentration, the length of H1 and incubation time of Au-probe (Fig. S1 in the Supplementary material). The optimizal condition was as follows, Mo2C concentration, 2.0 mg mL−1; the length of H1, 30 bp; incubation time of Au-probe, 60 min.

To investigate the selectivity of the PEC biosensor, miRNA-162b, miRNA-395b, miRNA-164d, miRNA-156b were chosen as interferants. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the photocurrent response of the biosensor for miRNA-159b was significantly higher than the response for the interferant miRNAs. Further, the PEC response of the biosensor towards a miRNA mixture containing miRNA-159b and the other interferants was almost identical to the response to miRNA-159b. The result demonstrates conclusively that the biosensor had a high selectivity for miRNA-159b. Similarly, in the presence of miRNA-159b (5 pM), the biosensor displayed excellent selectivity for miRNA-166a (Fig. 4B). The data in Fig. 4A and 4B confirmed the excellent specificity of the biosensor toward target miRNA.

The reproducibility of the PEC biosensor was assessed with a view towards practical usefulness. (RSD). Six biosensors were fabricated independently with a microRNA-159b concentration of 5 pM and a miRNA-166a concentration of 0.5 fM (Fig. 4C). The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the photocurrent for the six biosensors was only 2.7%, indicating that the proposed biosensor displayed remarkable reproducibility.

The stability of the biosensor was also investigated. Over 10 light on and light off cycles in 400 s, no obvious change in the measured PEC signal response was observed (Fig. 4D), and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is 2.16%, indicating that the signal was stable. To further validate the stability of the developed biosensor, a 2-week storage trial was performed. After storage in a refrigerator at 4 °C for 2 weeks, the biosensor retained 90% of its initial response, indicating that the biosensor possessed good stability.
See Supplementary materials.

In summary, energy transfer between CQDs and AuNPs was successfully exploited in a PEC system for the first time, enabling the ultrasensitive detection of dual microRNAs. The fabricated biosensor successfully coupled the high photostability, good biocompatibility, environment friendliness and easy functionalization of CQDs with the excellent electron transfer ability of Mo2C nanotubes to enhance the PEC response of CQDs. PEC quenching via energy transfer from CQDs to AuNP-labelled H1 and H2 probes, together with DSN-assisted signal amplification, allowed fast and ultrasensitive detection of miRNA-159b and miRNA-166a. Results offer a promising new strategy for detecting multiple miRNAs at ultralow levels.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province of China (No. ZR2018MB028), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21775090, 21375079), Founds of Shandong “Double Tops” Program (SYL2017XTTD15). GINW acknowledges funding support from the Dodd-Walls Centre for Photonic and Quantum Technologies.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2019.02.067.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Origins and mechanisms of miRNAs and siRNAs
Carbodiimide-mediated cross-linking of RNA to nylon membranes improves the detection of siRNA, miRNA and piRNA by northern blot
Systematic comparison of microarray profiling, real-time PCR, and next-generation sequencing technologies for measuring differential microRNA expression
Label-free platform for microRNA detection based on the fluorescence quenching of positively charged gold nanoparticles to silver nanoclusters
Signal-on electrochemiluminescence biosensor for microRNA-319a detection based on two-stage isothermal strand-displacement polymerase reaction
Bi-directional DNA walking machine and its application in an enzyme-free electrochemiluminescence biosensor for sensitive detection of microRNAs
Ratiometric biosensor array for multiplexed detection of microRNAs based on electrochemiluminescence coupled with cyclic voltammetry
Photoelectrochemical biosensor for microRNA detection based on multiple amplification strategies
Photoelectrochemical immunosensor for microRNA detection based on gold nanoparticles-functionalized g-C3N4 and anti-DNA:RNA antibody
Label-free, ultrasensitive and electrochemical immunosensing platform for microRNA detection using anti-DNA:RNA hybrid antibody and enzymatic signal amplification
Two-stage cyclic enzymatic amplification method for ultrasensitive electrochemical assay of microRNA-21 in the blood serum of gastric cancer patients
Label-free and enzyme-free homogeneous electrochemical biosensing strategy based on hybridization chain reaction: a facile, sensitive, and highly specific microRNA assay
Amperometric detection of microRNA based on DNA-controlled current of a molybdophosphate redox probe and amplification via hybridization chain reaction
A reusable potassium ion biosensor based on electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer
Quantum dots-fluorescence resonance energy transfer-based nanosensors and their application
Principles and applications of photoelectrochemical sensing strategies based on biofunctionalized nanostructures
Exciton-plasmon interactions between CdS quantum dots and Ag nanoparticles in photoelectrochemical system and its biosensing application

Resonance energy transfer from CdTe quantum dots to gold nanorods using MWCNTs/rGO nanoribbons as efficient signal amplifier for fabricating visible-light-driven “on-off-on” photoelectrochemical acetamiprid aptasensor
Energy transfer between CdS quantum dots and Au nanoparticles in photoelectrochemical detection
Highly sensitive photoelectrochemical assay for DNA methyltransferase activity and inhibitor screening by exciton energy transfer coupled with enzyme cleavage biosensing strategy
Invoking direct exciton–plasmon interactions by catalytic Ag deposition on Au nanoparticles: photoelectrochemical bioanalysis with high efficiency
A general approach to 3D porous CQDs/MxOy (M=Co, Ni) for remarkable performance hybrid supercapacitors
Label-free photoelectrochemical cytosensing via resonance energy transfer using gold nanoparticle-enhanced carbon dots
An electrochemically-switched BPEI-CQD/PPy/PSS membrane for selective separation of dilute copper ions from wastewater
Accelerated photocatalytic degradation of diclofenac by a novel CQDs/BiOCOOH hybrid material under visible-light irradiation: dechloridation, detoxicity, and a new superoxide radical model study
A carbon dot based biosensor for melamine detection by fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Amino-functionalized green fluorescent carbon dots as surface energy transfer biosensors for hyaluronidase
Silver nanoparticles modified two-dimensional transition metal carbides as nanocarriers to fabricate acetycholinesterase-based electrochemical biosensor
Transition metal carbides and nitrides in energy storage and conversion
Ultrafine molybdenum carbide nanoparticles composited with carbon as a highly active hydrogen-evolution electrocatalyst
Influence of graphitic/amorphous coated carbon on HER activity of low temperature synthesized β-Mo2C@C nanocomposites
In-situ carbonization for template-free synthesis of MoO2-Mo2C-C microspheres as high-performance lithium battery anode
Selective hydrogenation of lignin to produce chemical commodities by using a biochar supported Ni–Mo2C catalyst obtained from biomass
Effects of surface oxygen on charge storage in high surface area early transition-metal carbides and nitrides
